title: Password reset while user is currently logged in
preconditions: User has an active authenticated session in TaskFlow and navigates to the password reset page directly via URL
steps: Log in to TaskFlow with valid credentials,While the session is active, navigate directly to the password reset URL,Enter the currently registered email address,Submit the password reset form,Check whether the reset email is sent and observe system behavior
expected_result: The system should either redirect the already-authenticated user away from the reset page or handle the request gracefully without exposing session or account information, and should not allow a silent session takeover or duplicate reset link issuance without proper messaging.
rationale: An authenticated user triggering a password reset is an overlooked auth edge case that could expose session management vulnerabilities or allow account disruption by a third party who has temporary access to the browser.

title: Multiple concurrent password reset requests for the same account
preconditions: A registered user account exists in TaskFlow and the email service is operational
steps: Initiate a password reset request for a valid registered email address,Before the first reset link is used or expires, immediately initiate a second password reset request for the same email,Optionally initiate a third request in rapid succession (simulate concurrency),Receive all reset emails and attempt to use the first-issued reset link,Then attempt to use the second-issued reset link
expected_result: Only the most recently issued reset link should be valid. All previously issued links for the same account should be invalidated upon issuance of a new link. The system should not allow multiple active reset tokens simultaneously for the same account.
rationale: Concurrency in reset link generation is a common security gap. If multiple valid tokens coexist, a malicious actor who intercepts an older email could still reset the password, undermining security.

title: Password reset link accessed from a different browser or device than where it was requested
preconditions: A valid password reset link has been generated and sent to the user's email
steps: Request a password reset from Browser A on Device 1,Open the reset link from the email on a completely different browser (e.g., Browser B) on a different device (Device 2),Attempt to complete the password reset process by entering a new password,Submit the new password and observe the outcome
expected_result: The system should allow the password reset to complete successfully regardless of the browser or device, as reset links should be stateless tokens not bound to a specific session, browser, or device. The user should be informed of the successful reset.
rationale: Reset links sent via email must be device- and browser-agnostic. Binding tokens to a session or browser fingerprint would break legitimate use cases and is a common implementation mistake.

title: Password reset email delivery to an address with special characters or an alias format
preconditions: A registered TaskFlow account exists using an email address containing special characters or plus-sign aliasing (e.g., user+alias@domain.co.uk or user.name+tag@sub.domain.com)
steps: Navigate to the password reset page,Enter the registered email address containing special characters such as a plus sign, dots, or a subdomain (e.g., user+taskflow@domain.com),Submit the reset request,Check whether the reset email is delivered to the correct inbox,Attempt to use the delivered reset link to complete the password reset
expected_result: The system should correctly parse, store, and process email addresses with valid RFC 5321 special characters. The reset email should be delivered successfully, and the reset flow should complete without error.
rationale: Email addresses with plus signs or subdomains are valid per RFC standards but are frequently mishandled by input sanitization or regex validation logic, causing silent failures in the reset flow for a subset of users.

title: Password reset on mobile responsive web — layout and functionality integrity
preconditions: User accesses the password reset flow via a mobile browser (e.g., viewport width ≤ 375px). The web application is expected to be responsive as per REQ-2 scope.
steps: Open a mobile browser (or emulate a 375px viewport) and navigate to the password reset URL.,Verify the reset form renders correctly without horizontal overflow or broken layout.,Enter a valid registered email address in the email field.,Submit the form and follow the reset link received via email.,Complete the password reset flow entirely on the mobile viewport.
expected_result: The entire password reset flow (form submission, confirmation message, reset link page, and new password entry) is fully usable and correctly rendered on a mobile-sized viewport with no broken UI elements or inaccessible controls.
rationale: REQ-2 explicitly states mobile native apps are out of scope but the web app must be responsive. The existing tests do not cover responsive/mobile rendering of the password reset flow, which is a critical in-scope requirement.

title: Password reset email field submitted with whitespace-only input
preconditions: User is on the password reset page. The system handles user authentication as part of its in-scope features.
steps: Navigate to the password reset page.,Enter one or more space characters (e.g., '   ') into the email address field.,Submit the password reset form.
expected_result: The system treats whitespace-only input as invalid, does not attempt to look up or send a reset email, and displays an appropriate validation error message (e.g., 'Please enter a valid email address'). No reset email is dispatched.
rationale: The existing empty-field test covers a completely blank submission, but whitespace-only input is a distinct boundary case that can bypass naive empty-string checks and potentially trigger unexpected backend behavior or errors.

title: Concurrent simultaneous password reset requests for the same account
preconditions: A valid registered user account exists. The system supports password reset via email.
steps: Simultaneously submit two or more password reset requests for the same registered email address (e.g., via parallel browser sessions or automated concurrent HTTP requests).,Capture all reset links delivered to the email address.,Attempt to use the first received reset link to set a new password.,After successfully using the first link, attempt to use the second (or subsequent) reset link.
expected_result: The system handles concurrent requests gracefully: only the most recently generated token should be valid (or all tokens except the first used are invalidated upon first use). Earlier or unused tokens are rejected. No race condition leads to multiple active valid tokens or account data corruption.
rationale: Concurrency issues in token generation are not covered by existing tests. A race condition could result in multiple simultaneously valid reset tokens, posing a security vulnerability within the authentication scope of REQ-2.

title: Password reset email field containing a malformed or injected email string
preconditions: User is on the password reset page. The backend processes the submitted email to query the user store.
steps: Navigate to the password reset page.,Enter a malformed or potentially malicious string into the email field, such as: "test@example.com<script>alert(1)</script>", "' OR '1'='1", or an extremely long string (e.g., 500+ characters).,Submit the password reset form.,Observe the system response and check server logs for errors or unintended behavior.
expected_result: The system validates and sanitizes the input, rejects the malformed email with a clear validation error, does not execute any injected content, does not expose stack traces or internal errors, and does not send any reset email. The application remains stable.
rationale: Existing tests only cover well-formed email inputs (valid, unregistered, or empty). Malformed or adversarial inputs targeting the authentication subsystem (in-scope per REQ-2) are a critical security edge case not currently tested.

title: Password reset request with empty email field
preconditions: User is on the password reset page and the email input field is empty
steps: Navigate to the password reset page,Leave the email input field completely empty,Click or submit the 'Reset Password' button
expected_result: The system should display a validation error message indicating that the email field cannot be empty, and no reset email should be sent. The form should not be submitted.
rationale: An empty email input is a common boundary/null case not covered by existing tests. The system must gracefully handle blank submissions rather than crashing or sending requests with null values.

title: Multiple concurrent password reset requests for the same email
preconditions: A valid registered user account exists in the system
steps: Open two separate browser sessions or API clients simultaneously,Submit a password reset request for the same valid registered email address from both sessions at nearly the same time,Capture and attempt to use both reset links received in the email
expected_result: Only one valid reset link should be active at a time. The first link should be invalidated when the second request is processed, or both links are valid but only the first one used successfully completes the reset while the second is then invalidated. No race condition or duplicate reset tokens should cause undefined behavior.
rationale: Concurrency edge case not covered by existing tests. Simultaneous reset requests for the same account could lead to race conditions, duplicate tokens, or security vulnerabilities if not handled properly.

title: Password reset email containing leading and trailing whitespace
preconditions: A valid registered user account exists with a known email address
steps: Navigate to the password reset page,Enter the registered email address with leading and/or trailing whitespace (e.g., '  user@example.com  '),Submit the password reset form
expected_result: The system should either trim the whitespace and successfully send the reset email to the valid address, or display a clear validation error. It should not silently fail or treat the padded input as an unregistered email.
rationale: Whitespace-padded inputs are a common boundary/formatting edge case. Existing tests cover invalid formats but not inputs that are technically valid after trimming, which can cause inconsistent behavior.

title: Password reset request when email service is unavailable or times out
preconditions: A valid registered user account exists; the outbound email/SMTP service is simulated as unavailable or configured to time out
steps: Simulate or configure the email delivery service to be unavailable or to time out,Navigate to the password reset page,Enter a valid registered email address,Submit the password reset form,Observe the system response and any user-facing feedback
expected_result: The system should display a meaningful error message informing the user that the email could not be sent and to try again later. It should not expose internal error details, and the failed request should be logged for monitoring purposes.
rationale: Failure/timeout scenario not covered by existing tests. External email service outages are realistic failure cases; the system must handle them gracefully without exposing sensitive internal errors to the user.

title: Password reset with empty/blank email field
preconditions: User is on the password reset request page and the system is operational.
steps: Navigate to the password reset page,Leave the email field completely empty (or enter only whitespace characters),Submit the password reset form
expected_result: The system rejects the submission with a clear validation error message (e.g., 'Email address is required') and does not attempt any backend processing or send any email.
rationale: Empty or whitespace-only inputs are a common boundary condition. The system must validate presence before format, and silently passing an empty field to the backend could cause unhandled null/empty exceptions.

title: Multiple concurrent password reset requests for the same account
preconditions: A registered user account exists. The system is operational.
steps: Send a password reset request for a valid registered email, generating reset link #1,Before link #1 expires or is used, send a second password reset request for the same email, generating reset link #2,Attempt to use reset link #1 to reset the password
expected_result: Reset link #1 is invalidated upon issuance of reset link #2. Only the most recently issued link (#2) remains valid. Using the older link #1 results in an appropriate error (e.g., 'This reset link is no longer valid').
rationale: Concurrency and token lifecycle management is a security-critical edge case. If stale tokens remain active, an attacker who intercepts an old email could still reset the account password even after the user requested a new link.

title: Password reset link accessed after account is deactivated or deleted
preconditions: A registered user has requested a valid password reset link. The account is subsequently deactivated or deleted before the link is used.
steps: User requests a password reset link, which is sent to their email,An administrator deactivates or deletes the user account,The user clicks the still-valid (non-expired) password reset link,User attempts to set a new password
expected_result: The system detects that the associated account no longer exists or is inactive and denies the reset operation with an appropriate error message. No password change is persisted.
rationale: This is an auth/permission edge case where a token is technically valid but the underlying account state has changed. Allowing password reset for a deleted/deactivated account could reactivate it unintentionally or cause undefined system behavior.

title: Password reset email sending failure due to mail service timeout or unavailability
preconditions: A valid registered email is used. The outbound email/SMTP service is unavailable or times out.
steps: Simulate or configure the mail service to be unavailable or to time out,Navigate to the password reset page,Enter a valid registered email address,Submit the password reset form
expected_result: The system handles the failure gracefully, returns a user-friendly error message indicating the reset email could not be sent, and does not expose internal error details. No partial or inconsistent state (e.g., a persisted but undeliverable token) is left in the database.
rationale: Failure/timeout scenarios for downstream services are frequently overlooked. If the email service fails silently, the user receives no feedback and may be locked out of recovery, while orphaned reset tokens could accumulate in the database.

title: Registration with password exceeding maximum allowed length boundary
preconditions: The registration page is accessible and the system enforces a maximum password length
steps: Navigate to the registration page,Enter a valid, unregistered email address,Enter a password that exceeds the maximum allowed length (e.g., 256+ characters),Submit the registration form
expected_result: The system rejects the registration attempt and displays a clear error message indicating the password exceeds the maximum allowed length, without crashing or truncating silently
rationale: While the minimum boundary is tested, the maximum boundary is not. Silent truncation or acceptance of excessively long passwords can lead to security vulnerabilities and inconsistent authentication behavior.

title: Registration with email address containing leading/trailing whitespace
preconditions: The registration page is accessible and no account exists for the target email
steps: Navigate to the registration page,Enter an email address with leading and/or trailing whitespace (e.g., '  user@example.com  '),Enter a valid password meeting all requirements,Submit the registration form
expected_result: The system either trims the whitespace and registers the user successfully with the normalized email, or rejects the input with a clear validation error. The system must not create a duplicate account if the trimmed version already exists.
rationale: Whitespace in email inputs is a common user mistake. If not handled, it can create duplicate accounts, cause login failures, or allow bypass of the duplicate-email check, which is a critical data integrity issue.

title: Registration with a password composed entirely of whitespace characters
preconditions: The registration page is accessible
steps: Navigate to the registration page,Enter a valid, unregistered email address,Enter a password consisting only of spaces or whitespace characters (e.g., '        ') that meets the minimum length requirement,Submit the registration form
expected_result: The system rejects the registration attempt and returns a validation error indicating that the password cannot consist solely of whitespace characters, preventing creation of an insecure account
rationale: A whitespace-only password may pass a simple length check but represents an extremely weak credential. This edge case tests whether the system performs semantic password validation beyond length, preventing a significant security vulnerability.

title: Concurrent registration attempts with the same email address
preconditions: The registration endpoint is accessible; the target email address is not yet registered in the system
steps: Simultaneously submit two or more registration requests with the same email address and valid passwords (e.g., using parallel API calls or automated scripts),Observe the system response for each concurrent request,Check the database to verify the number of accounts created for that email
expected_result: Exactly one account is created for the email address. All subsequent concurrent requests receive a duplicate-email error. No race condition results in multiple accounts being registered for the same email.
rationale: Race conditions during concurrent registrations can bypass the duplicate-email check, resulting in multiple accounts for a single email. This undermines authentication integrity and is not covered by the sequential duplicate-email test.

title: Password consisting entirely of spaces with numbers and special characters
preconditions: User is on the password creation or reset form. Password field accepts input.
steps: Enter a password consisting of spaces, a number, and a special character totaling 8+ characters (e.g., '   1!   '),Submit the password form
expected_result: The system should reject the password as invalid, since whitespace-only or whitespace-padded passwords are not secure, or enforce a policy that disallows leading/trailing/only spaces.
rationale: Whitespace characters may satisfy length and character-class rules technically, but represent a serious security vulnerability. This edge case verifies the system handles whitespace characters appropriately and does not accept them as valid complexity fillers.

title: Password at maximum boundary length with Unicode or multi-byte special characters
preconditions: User is on the password creation or reset form. The system supports Unicode input.
steps: Enter a password that is exactly 8 characters long but uses a multi-byte Unicode character (e.g., '©' or '€') as the special character (e.g., 'Pass1©ab'),Submit the password form,Verify whether the Unicode character is counted as 1 character or multiple bytes toward the length requirement
expected_result: The system should correctly count the Unicode character as one character toward the length requirement and accept or reject it consistently based on whether it qualifies as a 'special character' per the defined policy.
rationale: Multi-byte Unicode characters can cause inconsistencies in character counting between front-end and back-end validation. This could allow passwords that appear valid to fail back-end checks or vice versa, creating security gaps or poor user experience.

title: Password containing only numbers and special characters with no alphabetic characters
preconditions: User is on the password creation or reset form.
steps: Enter a password of 8+ characters containing only digits and special characters and no letters (e.g., '12345!@#'),Submit the password form
expected_result: The system should evaluate whether its policy requires alphabetic characters. If no alphabetic requirement exists, the password should be accepted since it meets length, number, and special character requirements. The result should be consistent with documented policy.
rationale: The requirement only specifies a number and a special character but does not explicitly require letters. A password of all digits and symbols technically meets the stated criteria. This edge case verifies that validation logic does not silently enforce undocumented requirements, ensuring alignment between spec and implementation.

title: Null or empty string submitted as password
preconditions: User is on the password creation or reset form. The form can be submitted programmatically or via API to bypass front-end validation.
steps: Submit a null value or an empty string as the password via direct API call (bypassing front-end required field validation),Observe the system response
expected_result: The system should return a clear validation error indicating the password does not meet minimum requirements, without throwing an unhandled exception, 500 error, or exposing internal system details.
rationale: Null and empty inputs are classic failure-injection scenarios. Even if front-end validation blocks empty passwords, API-level submission of null/empty values must be handled gracefully by the back end to prevent system crashes, data corruption, or security information leakage.

title: Verify behavior when verification link is accessed at exactly the 24-hour boundary (T+24:00:00)
preconditions: A verification email has been issued. The system clock is controllable or mockable. The link has not been used.
steps: Request a password reset to trigger a verification email,Record the exact timestamp of link issuance,Advance the system clock to exactly T+24:00:00 (to the second of issuance),Attempt to use the verification link at the exact expiry moment
expected_result: The link is treated as expired at exactly T+24:00:00 and the system rejects it with an appropriate 'link expired' error message, confirming the boundary is inclusive (expires at, not after, 24 hours).
rationale: The existing test only checks 'after 24 hours' and 'just before' expiry, but does not test the exact boundary second. Off-by-one or inclusive/exclusive boundary logic errors are common and could leave a security gap or falsely reject valid links.

title: Verify that a second password reset request invalidates the previously issued unexpired link
preconditions: A user account exists. No pending verification links are present.
steps: Submit a password reset request for the user account — note Link A is issued,Without using Link A, submit a second password reset request for the same account — note Link B is issued,Attempt to use Link A (the first, still within 24-hour window),Attempt to use Link B (the second link)
expected_result: Link A is invalidated upon issuance of Link B and returns an appropriate error (e.g., 'link no longer valid'). Link B is accepted and allows password reset. Only one active link per user should be valid at any time.
rationale: Multiple outstanding valid links for the same account represent a security risk. This scenario is not covered by existing tests, which only verify uniqueness and single-use but not mutual invalidation upon re-request.

title: Verify system behavior when the verification link is malformed or tampered with
preconditions: A valid verification email has been issued and the link token is known.
steps: Request a password reset and receive the verification email,Extract the unique token from the link,Modify the token by altering one character (e.g., increment last character),Submit the tampered link to the verification endpoint,Also attempt submitting a completely empty or null token value to the endpoint
expected_result: The system rejects the tampered link with an error such as 'invalid or unrecognized link' without revealing whether a valid token exists. An empty/null token submission returns a 400 Bad Request or equivalent error. No account access is granted.
rationale: Tampered and empty tokens are not covered by existing tests. This is a critical security edge case to prevent token enumeration or brute-force attacks against the verification endpoint.

title: Verify verification link behavior when the associated user account is deleted or deactivated after link issuance
preconditions: A user account exists and is active. A valid, unexpired verification link has been issued for that account.
steps: Request a password reset for an active user account — a verification link is issued,Deactivate or delete the user account before the link is used,Attempt to use the verification link within the 24-hour window
expected_result: The system detects that the associated account no longer exists or is inactive and rejects the link with an appropriate error. No password reset is performed and no sensitive information about the account state is leaked.
rationale: Existing tests assume the account remains valid for the link lifetime. Account deletion or deactivation between link issuance and use is an unhandled edge case that could cause unexpected system behavior or expose account state information.

title: Login attempt with email address exceeding maximum length boundary
preconditions: System is operational. A registered user account exists with a valid email and password.
steps: Navigate to the login page,Enter an email address that exceeds the maximum allowed length (e.g., 255+ characters) in the email field,Enter a valid password in the password field,Click the login/submit button
expected_result: The system should reject the input gracefully with an appropriate validation error message (e.g., 'Email address is too long') without crashing, exposing stack traces, or causing unexpected behavior.
rationale: Boundary value testing on input length is not covered by existing tests. Oversized inputs can cause buffer overflows, database errors, or unexpected system behavior if not properly validated.

title: Login attempt with correct credentials after multiple consecutive failed login attempts (account lockout)
preconditions: System is operational. A registered user account exists. The system has a configured account lockout policy (e.g., after 5 failed attempts).
steps: Navigate to the login page,Attempt to log in with the registered email and an incorrect password repeatedly until the lockout threshold is reached (e.g., 5 times),Observe whether the account is locked,Attempt to log in using the correct email and correct password after the lockout is triggered
expected_result: After exceeding the failed login threshold, the account should be temporarily locked and the user should NOT be able to log in even with correct credentials. An appropriate message (e.g., 'Account temporarily locked. Please try again later or reset your password.') should be displayed.
rationale: Brute-force attack prevention is a critical security concern not covered by existing tests. The system must enforce lockout policies to prevent unauthorized access via credential stuffing or brute-force attacks.

title: Login attempt with email containing mixed-case characters (case sensitivity check)
preconditions: A registered user account exists with the email 'User@Example.COM' (or any mixed-case email). The user knows their correct password.
steps: Navigate to the login page,Enter the registered email in all lowercase (e.g., 'user@example.com') in the email field,Enter the correct password,Click the login/submit button,Repeat the test entering the email in all uppercase (e.g., 'USER@EXAMPLE.COM'),Repeat the test entering the email with random mixed case (e.g., 'uSeR@eXaMpLe.CoM')
expected_result: The system should treat email addresses as case-insensitive per RFC 5321 standards and allow successful login regardless of the case used in the email field, as long as the password is correct.
rationale: Email case sensitivity is a common edge case not covered by existing tests. Inconsistent case handling can lock users out of their accounts or cause duplicate account issues.

title: Login attempt using a valid session token after the user has reset their password
preconditions: A registered user is currently logged in with an active session token. The user initiates a password reset via email (as described in the related context requirement) and successfully changes their password.
steps: Log in as a registered user and capture/note the active session token,Initiate and complete a password reset flow via email to change the account password,Attempt to access the system or perform an action using the previously captured (old) session token,Alternatively, attempt to log in using the old password after the reset
expected_result: All previously active sessions and tokens should be invalidated upon a successful password reset. Attempts to use old session tokens should be rejected with an authentication error (e.g., redirect to login page). Login using the old password should also fail.
rationale: Session invalidation after a password reset is a critical security edge case not covered by existing tests. Failure to invalidate old sessions after a password change can allow unauthorized parties who previously obtained a session token to maintain access.

title: Failed attempt counter behavior when lockout period expires without successful login
preconditions: User account exists. User has triggered account lockout with 5 consecutive failed attempts. The 15-minute lockout period has just elapsed.
steps: Perform 5 consecutive failed login attempts to lock the account,Wait for the 15-minute lockout period to expire,Perform 4 additional failed login attempts,Verify the failed attempt counter state,Perform 1 more failed login attempt (5th since unlock),Check whether the account is locked again
expected_result: After the lockout period expires, the failed attempt counter resets to 0. The account is re-locked after 5 new consecutive failed attempts post-unlock, not after fewer attempts carrying over from before the lockout.
rationale: It is ambiguous whether the counter resets upon lockout expiry or only upon successful login. A residual counter after unlock could incorrectly lock the account in fewer than 5 attempts, violating the requirement.

title: Concurrent simultaneous failed login attempts race condition at the 5th attempt boundary
preconditions: User account exists and is unlocked. The failed attempt counter is at 4 (one attempt away from lockout).
steps: Perform 4 consecutive failed login attempts to bring the counter to 4,Simultaneously fire 3 concurrent failed login requests for the same account using parallel threads or API calls,Observe whether the account is locked exactly once, multiple times, or enters an inconsistent state,Attempt a login after the concurrent requests complete and verify account status
expected_result: The system handles concurrent requests atomically. The account is locked exactly once after the 5th failed attempt, with no race condition causing the counter to be incremented incorrectly, bypassed, or resulting in duplicate lockout events.
rationale: Concurrent login attempts at the lockout boundary can expose race conditions in counter increment logic, potentially allowing more than 5 failed attempts before locking or causing undefined account states.

title: Failed attempt counter does not reset after a failed password reset flow
preconditions: User account exists. User has accumulated 3 consecutive failed login attempts. Password reset via email feature is available per related context.
steps: Perform 3 consecutive failed login attempts,Initiate a password reset request via email without completing the full reset,Abandon the password reset flow (e.g., do not click the email link),Perform 2 more failed login attempts,Verify whether the account is now locked
expected_result: An incomplete or abandoned password reset flow does not reset the failed login attempt counter. The account is locked after the 5th cumulative consecutive failed login attempt, preserving the count from before the reset attempt.
rationale: A user could exploit an incomplete password reset to artificially clear the failed attempt counter and evade account lockout, bypassing the brute-force protection mechanism.

title: Lockout duration boundary — login attempt at exactly 15 minutes (boundary precision)
preconditions: User account is locked due to 5 consecutive failed login attempts. A mechanism to measure time elapsed since lockout is available.
steps: Record the exact timestamp when the account is locked,Attempt to login with correct credentials at exactly 14 minutes and 59 seconds after lockout,Verify the attempt is rejected,Attempt to login with correct credentials at exactly 15 minutes and 0 seconds after lockout,Verify the outcome of this boundary attempt
expected_result: Login is rejected at 14 minutes 59 seconds. At exactly 15 minutes, the account is unlocked and a login with correct credentials succeeds. The system must not have off-by-one timing errors (e.g., unlocking at 14:59 or remaining locked beyond 15:00).
rationale: The exact 15-minute boundary is a classic off-by-one timing edge case. Imprecise timer implementations (e.g., rounding, truncation, clock drift) could unlock accounts early or keep them locked longer than specified.

title: Password reset with empty/blank email field
preconditions: User is on the password reset page and the email input field is visible
steps: Navigate to the password reset page,Leave the email field completely empty,Click the 'Reset Password' or 'Submit' button,Repeat the test with a whitespace-only input (e.g., spaces or tabs)
expected_result: The system should display a validation error indicating that the email field is required and must not be empty or blank. No reset email should be dispatched and no server-side processing should occur.
rationale: Empty or whitespace-only inputs are a fundamental boundary condition not covered by existing tests. Failing to handle this could cause unhandled exceptions, unnecessary server calls, or security information leakage.

title: Password reset link used concurrently in multiple sessions simultaneously
preconditions: A valid, unused password reset link has been generated and is within its expiry window. Two separate browser sessions or clients are available.
steps: Generate a password reset link for a registered user,Open the reset link simultaneously in two different browser sessions or HTTP clients,Submit a new password in both sessions at approximately the same time (within milliseconds),Observe which session succeeds and whether the second session is properly rejected
expected_result: Only one password reset should succeed. The second concurrent attempt should be rejected with an appropriate error (e.g., 'link already used' or 'token invalid'). No race condition should allow both resets to complete or leave the account in an inconsistent state.
rationale: Concurrent use of the same reset token is a concurrency edge case not addressed by existing tests. A race condition here could allow token reuse or result in unpredictable account state, posing a security risk.

title: Password reset requested multiple times in rapid succession for the same account
preconditions: A registered user account exists. The user has access to the password reset page.
steps: Submit a password reset request for a valid registered email,Without waiting, immediately submit another password reset request for the same email,Submit a third reset request in rapid succession,Check the user's inbox for multiple reset emails,Attempt to use the first-issued reset link after newer links have been generated
expected_result: The system should either invalidate all previously issued tokens when a new request is made (making only the latest link valid), or apply rate limiting to prevent abuse. Earlier reset links should not remain valid once superseded. The user should receive clear guidance if rate limiting is enforced.
rationale: Multiple rapid reset requests for the same account are not covered by existing tests. This could lead to token flooding attacks, inbox spam, or old tokens remaining valid alongside new ones, which is a security vulnerability.

title: Password reset email contains special characters or is an internationalized (IDN) email address
preconditions: The system has a registered account using an email address with special characters or an internationalized domain (e.g., user+tag@example.com, üser@münchen.de, or user@xn--mnchen-3ya.de).
steps: Navigate to the password reset page,Enter a registered email address containing a '+' tag (e.g., user+alias@example.com),Submit the reset request and verify a reset email is received,Repeat with an email address using an internationalized domain name (IDN),Attempt to complete the reset flow end-to-end for each variant
expected_result: The system should correctly handle valid but non-standard email formats such as tagged addresses and IDN domains. A reset email should be sent and the full reset flow should complete successfully without errors or silent failures.
rationale: Existing invalid-format tests cover clearly malformed emails, but do not cover valid edge-case formats like tagged addresses or internationalized domains. Mishandling these could block legitimate users from resetting their passwords.

title: Invite team member with an email address at maximum allowed length
preconditions: User is logged in as an organization administrator. The system has a defined maximum email length (e.g., 254 characters per RFC 5321).
steps: Log in as an organization administrator.,Navigate to the 'Invite Team Member' feature.,Enter an email address exactly at the maximum allowed length (e.g., 254 characters total, with a valid format such as 'aaa...aaa@domain.com').,Submit the invitation.
expected_result: The system successfully sends the invitation to the maximum-length email address without truncation or error, and a confirmation message is displayed.
rationale: Boundary value at the maximum email length is not covered by existing tests. Emails at the RFC-defined limit may cause buffer overflows, truncation, or silent failures in some implementations.

title: Invite team member with an email domain that does not exist or fails DNS resolution
preconditions: User is logged in as an organization administrator. Network is available but the target email domain (e.g., 'user@nonexistentdomain12345xyz.com') has no DNS record.
steps: Log in as an organization administrator.,Navigate to the 'Invite Team Member' feature.,Enter a syntactically valid email address with a non-existent domain (e.g., 'testuser@nonexistentdomain12345xyz.com').,Submit the invitation.,Observe the system response immediately and after any async processing.
expected_result: The system either validates the domain at submission time and displays an appropriate error, or queues the invitation and notifies the admin of delivery failure, without crashing or silently discarding the invite.
rationale: A syntactically valid but undeliverable email address bypasses format validation. This edge case tests whether the system handles delivery failures gracefully and communicates them to the administrator.

title: Rapid duplicate invitation submissions by admin (concurrent/double-click scenario)
preconditions: User is logged in as an organization administrator. A valid, uninvited email address is available.
steps: Log in as an organization administrator.,Navigate to the 'Invite Team Member' feature.,Enter a valid, uninvited email address.,Click or trigger the 'Send Invitation' action multiple times in rapid succession (simulating a double-click or concurrent API calls).,Observe the number of invitation emails received and system state.
expected_result: Only one invitation is sent to the target email address. The system prevents duplicate invitations from being created due to rapid repeated submissions, and the admin receives a single confirmation.
rationale: Concurrency and double-submission are not covered by existing tests. Without idempotency controls, rapid resubmission could flood the invitee with duplicate emails or create duplicate pending invite records.

title: Invite a user whose email matches an existing pending (unaccepted) invitation
preconditions: User is logged in as an organization administrator. A prior invitation has been sent to 'pendinguser@example.com' but has not yet been accepted or expired.
steps: Log in as an organization administrator.,Navigate to the 'Invite Team Member' feature.,Enter the email address 'pendinguser@example.com' that already has a pending invitation.,Submit the invitation.,Observe the system response and check whether a new invitation token is generated.
expected_result: The system informs the administrator that an invitation is already pending for this email. It either blocks the duplicate invite or provides an option to resend/replace the existing invitation, without creating conflicting invitation tokens.
rationale: Existing tests cover re-inviting a user who is already a full member, but not one with a pending invite. This scenario could result in multiple valid invitation tokens, security issues, or confusion for the invitee.

title: Verify behavior when a user is simultaneously assigned multiple conflicting roles
preconditions: A user account exists in the system and can be assigned roles by an Administrator
steps: Log in as Administrator,Navigate to user management for a specific user,Assign both 'Contributor' and 'Project Manager' roles to the same user simultaneously,Save the role assignments,Log in as that user,Attempt to perform actions exclusive to Administrator, Project Manager, and Contributor roles respectively
expected_result: The system should either prevent assigning conflicting/multiple roles or apply a well-defined precedence rule (e.g., least privileged or highest privilege wins). The resulting effective permissions should be deterministic and documented, with no permission bleed between roles.
rationale: When a user holds multiple roles, permission conflicts can arise, leading to unintended privilege escalation or denial of legitimate access. This scenario is not covered by existing tests which only verify single-role assignments.

title: Verify that a Project Manager cannot grant themselves or another user Administrator-level permissions
preconditions: A Project Manager account and a Contributor account exist in the system
steps: Log in as a Project Manager,Navigate to user management or role assignment settings,Attempt to elevate own role to Administrator,Attempt to assign Administrator role to the Contributor account,Attempt to modify permissions of the Administrator role definition
expected_result: All privilege escalation attempts by the Project Manager should be denied with an appropriate authorization error. No role changes should persist in the system.
rationale: Existing tests only verify that a Contributor cannot self-escalate. The Project Manager role — having broader permissions than a Contributor — presents a higher-risk vector for horizontal or vertical privilege escalation that is not yet tested.

title: Verify permissions remain enforced after a role is downgraded mid-session
preconditions: A user is actively logged in with Project Manager role; an Administrator account exists
steps: Log in as the target user with Project Manager role and start a session,In a separate Administrator session, downgrade the target user's role to Contributor,Without logging out, attempt to perform a Project Manager-exclusive action in the target user's active session,Also attempt to access a previously accessible project management page via direct URL
expected_result: The system should enforce the updated role in real-time or upon the next permission check, denying the downgraded user access to Project Manager-exclusive actions even within the same session. Cached permissions should not allow continued access.
rationale: Role changes applied during an active session may not be enforced if permissions are cached at login. This session-persistence edge case could lead to unauthorized access and is not covered by any existing tests.

title: Verify system behavior when a role definition is deleted while users are still assigned to it
preconditions: A custom or standard role has active users assigned to it; Administrator account is available
steps: Log in as Administrator,Identify a role (e.g., Project Manager) that has at least one active user assigned,Attempt to delete or deactivate the role from the system,Observe what happens to currently assigned users,Attempt to log in as a user who was assigned the deleted role,Check what permissions that user has after the role deletion
expected_result: The system should either prevent deletion of a role with active assignments and show a meaningful error, or safely reassign affected users to a default/no-role state with a notification. Users previously assigned the deleted role must not retain any stale permissions or fall back to an undefined privileged state.
rationale: Deleting a role while users are assigned to it is a boundary condition that can cause orphaned permission states or security gaps. This scenario is entirely absent from existing tests and could result in unpredictable access control behavior.

title: Administrator attempts to deactivate their own account
preconditions: A user with Administrator role is logged in and has access to account management functionality.
steps: Log in as an Administrator user.,Navigate to the user account management section.,Locate the currently authenticated Administrator's own account.,Attempt to deactivate the Administrator's own account.
expected_result: The system shall prevent the Administrator from deactivating their own account and return a clear error message (e.g., 'You cannot deactivate your own account'), leaving the account in its active state.
rationale: Self-deactivation by the only active Administrator could cause a system lockout with no privileged account able to restore access. This is a critical edge case not covered by existing tests.

title: Deactivated user's active session is invalidated immediately upon deactivation
preconditions: Target user is logged in with one or more active sessions (e.g., web and mobile). Administrator account is available and active.
steps: Log in as the target user and establish an active session (obtain a valid session token or JWT).,In a separate browser/client, log in as an Administrator.,As Administrator, deactivate the target user account.,Immediately attempt to perform an authenticated action using the target user's previously valid session token.,Attempt to log in again as the deactivated user using valid credentials.
expected_result: All existing sessions for the deactivated user are invalidated immediately. Any API call or page request using the old session token returns a 401 Unauthorized response. A new login attempt with valid credentials is also rejected. No grace period is allowed.
rationale: The requirement states access must be revoked 'immediately'. Existing tests verify deactivation occurs but may not confirm that in-flight, concurrent, or pre-existing authenticated sessions are forcibly terminated — a critical security gap.

title: Administrator deactivates user account while the user is mid-password-reset flow
preconditions: A target user has initiated a password reset and possesses a valid, unexpired password reset token sent via email. Administrator account is available and active.
steps: As the target user, trigger a password reset and obtain a valid reset token from the email.,As Administrator, deactivate the target user's account before the reset token is used.,As the target user, attempt to complete the password reset using the previously obtained valid token.,Attempt to log in with the newly set password after the reset flow completes.
expected_result: The password reset token is invalidated upon account deactivation. Attempting to use the token returns an error (e.g., 'Reset link is invalid or expired'). Even if the password is changed, login with the new credentials is denied because the account is deactivated.
rationale: This tests the intersection of REQ-011 and the password reset context. A deactivated user must not be able to regain access through an orphaned password reset token, which represents a potential privilege-escalation bypass.

title: Concurrent deactivation requests for the same user account by two Administrators
preconditions: Two Administrator accounts are active. One target active user account exists.
steps: Log in simultaneously as two different Administrators (Admin A and Admin B).,Both Administrators navigate to the same target user account at the same time.,Both Administrators submit a deactivation request for the same user account simultaneously (concurrent HTTP requests).,Inspect the system's response to both requests.,Verify the final state of the target user account and audit log.
expected_result: The system handles the concurrent requests idempotently. The user account is deactivated exactly once. Both requests receive a valid response (e.g., the first succeeds with 200 OK, the second returns a meaningful status such as 200 OK or 409 Conflict indicating already deactivated). No duplicate audit log entries or data corruption occurs.
rationale: Concurrent deactivation requests from multiple Administrators are a race condition not covered by existing tests. Without proper locking or idempotency handling, this could lead to inconsistent account state or duplicate audit events.

title: Update display name with special characters and Unicode/emoji input
preconditions: User is authenticated and on the profile update page
steps: Navigate to the profile update form,Enter a display name containing special characters and Unicode, e.g., '<script>alert(1)</script>', '日本語名前', or '😀🔥',Submit the profile update form,Retrieve and display the updated profile
expected_result: The system either accepts valid Unicode/emoji display names and stores/renders them safely (with XSS payloads sanitized), or returns a clear validation error. No raw HTML or script execution should occur in any rendered context.
rationale: Existing tests cover length and empty string but not Unicode, emoji, or injection-style special characters. These inputs can expose XSS vulnerabilities or cause rendering/storage issues not covered by standard boundary tests.

title: Upload an avatar image that exceeds the maximum allowed file size
preconditions: User is authenticated; a valid image file (e.g., JPEG) larger than the documented maximum file size limit is available
steps: Navigate to the profile update page,Attempt to upload a valid image format (e.g., JPEG) whose file size exceeds the maximum allowed limit,Submit the form
expected_result: The system rejects the upload with a descriptive error message indicating the file size limit exceeded. No partial file is stored and other profile fields remain unchanged.
rationale: Existing tests only cover invalid image format, not oversized valid-format images. Oversized uploads can exhaust storage, cause timeouts, or bypass validation if only MIME type is checked.

title: Update time zone with an invalid or non-existent time zone identifier
preconditions: User is authenticated and on the profile update page
steps: Navigate to the profile update form,Submit a profile update request with a time zone value set to an invalid identifier (e.g., 'Invalid/Timezone', 'UTC+99', or an empty string injected via API bypass),Observe the system response and check the stored time zone value
expected_result: The system rejects the invalid time zone with a validation error and does not persist the invalid value. The user's existing time zone remains unchanged.
rationale: No existing test covers time zone validation. Accepting an invalid time zone identifier could cause downstream failures in scheduling, notifications, or display logic, and could be exploited via direct API calls bypassing UI dropdowns.

title: Concurrent profile updates from two simultaneous sessions for the same user
preconditions: User has two active authenticated sessions (e.g., two browser tabs or devices)
steps: In Session A, begin editing the display name to 'NameFromSessionA',In Session B, simultaneously submit a profile update changing the display name to 'NameFromSessionB' and the time zone to 'America/New_York',Submit the update from Session A immediately after Session B's submission,Retrieve the final saved profile state
expected_result: The system handles the concurrent updates without data corruption or partial writes (e.g., last-write-wins with full field integrity, or an optimistic locking conflict error is returned). The stored profile reflects a consistent, complete state and no fields from either update are silently lost or mixed.
rationale: Concurrent profile mutations are not covered by existing tests and can cause race conditions resulting in partial updates, lost writes, or inconsistent field combinations, which is a common concurrency edge case for user profile services.

title: Administrator attempts to create a user with a malformed or extremely long email address
preconditions: Administrator is logged in and on the user creation page.
steps: Navigate to the user management section.,Click 'Create New User'.,Enter an extremely long email address (e.g., 300+ characters) or a malformed email (e.g., 'user@@domain..com', 'plainaddress', '@missinglocal.com') in the email field.,Fill in all other required fields with valid data.,Click 'Submit' or 'Create User'.
expected_result: The system should reject the input with a clear, descriptive validation error message indicating the email format is invalid, without crashing or exposing internal errors. The form should remain populated with previously entered data to allow correction.
rationale: Malformed or excessively long email addresses are a common boundary/type-mismatch edge case not covered by existing tests. They can cause failures in downstream processes like password reset emails, and the system must handle them gracefully at the point of creation.

title: Administrator attempts to delete or deactivate their own account
preconditions: Administrator is logged in and has access to the user management section. The administrator's own account is visible in the user list.
steps: Navigate to the user management section.,Locate the currently logged-in administrator's own account in the user list.,Attempt to click 'Delete' or 'Deactivate' on their own account.,Confirm the action if a confirmation dialog appears.
expected_result: The system should prevent the administrator from deleting or deactivating their own active account, displaying a clear warning message such as 'You cannot remove your own administrator account.' The account should remain active and the administrator session should remain valid.
rationale: This is a critical permission/auth edge case not covered by existing tests. Allowing an admin to self-delete could result in a lockout scenario with no administrator remaining in the system, which directly undermines intuitive and safe user management.

title: Password reset email is triggered for a deactivated or non-existent user account
preconditions: Administrator is logged in. At least one user account exists in a deactivated/suspended state. The administrator has access to the password reset functionality.
steps: Navigate to the user management section.,Locate a deactivated or suspended user account.,Attempt to trigger a password reset email for the deactivated account.,Additionally, attempt to send a password reset to a manually typed non-existent email address.
expected_result: For a deactivated account, the system should display a clear warning that the account is inactive and the reset email will not be sent (or should indicate the action is blocked). For a non-existent email, the system should display an appropriate error. No actual email should be sent to avoid information leakage, and no system crash should occur.
rationale: The existing test for password reset only covers the happy path. Triggering resets for deactivated or non-existent users is a failure/boundary scenario that could expose user enumeration vulnerabilities or cause confusing administrator experiences.

title: Concurrent administrators simultaneously editing the same user account
preconditions: Two administrator accounts are active and logged in simultaneously. Both administrators navigate to the same user's detail/edit page.
steps: Admin A opens the edit page for User X and begins modifying the user's role.,Simultaneously, Admin B opens the edit page for the same User X and begins modifying the user's email address.,Admin A saves their changes first.,Admin B then saves their changes.
expected_result: The system should handle the concurrent edit gracefully, either by implementing optimistic locking (warning Admin B that the record was modified since they opened it and prompting them to review before saving) or by merging non-conflicting fields correctly. No data should be silently lost or corrupted, and the final state of the user record should be consistent and auditable.
rationale: This concurrency edge case is not covered by any existing tests. In multi-admin environments, simultaneous edits to the same user record are a realistic scenario that could lead to silent data loss, making user management unpredictable and unsafe.

title: Create a project with a name containing only whitespace characters
preconditions: User is authenticated and has project creation permissions. The project creation form is open.
steps: Navigate to the project creation screen,Enter a project name consisting entirely of whitespace characters (spaces, tabs, newlines),Fill in all other required fields with valid data,Submit the form
expected_result: The system should reject the submission and display a validation error indicating that the project name cannot be blank or consist only of whitespace. The project should not be created.
rationale: A name with only whitespace may pass a simple 'required field' check but is semantically empty. This boundary case between truly empty and visibly non-empty input is not covered by existing tests and could result in projects with invisible or meaningless names being created.

title: Attempt to create a project with a duplicate project name
preconditions: User is authenticated and has project creation permissions. A project named 'Alpha Project' already exists in the system.
steps: Navigate to the project creation screen,Enter the project name 'Alpha Project' (identical to an existing project),Fill in all other required fields with valid data,Submit the form
expected_result: The system should either reject the duplicate name with a clear error message indicating the name is already in use, or handle the duplicate gracefully per business rules (e.g., allow with a warning). The behavior must be deterministic and not result in silent data corruption.
rationale: None of the existing tests verify behavior when a duplicate project name is submitted. Duplicate names can cause confusion, broken references, or data integrity issues, making this a critical edge case for project management.

title: Attempt to edit a project by a user without edit permissions
preconditions: Two user accounts exist: one project owner (User A) and one read-only or non-member user (User B). A project created by User A exists in the system.
steps: Log in as User B (non-owner / read-only user),Navigate to the project details page for User A's project,Attempt to access the edit project functionality (via UI or direct API call),Submit modified project details
expected_result: The system should deny the edit request and return an appropriate authorization error (e.g., HTTP 403 Forbidden or equivalent UI message). No changes should be persisted to the project.
rationale: Existing tests only cover successful edits and field validation. Authorization enforcement on edit operations is a critical security edge case not currently tested, and failure here could allow unauthorized modification of project data.

title: Create a project with special characters and Unicode in the project name
preconditions: User is authenticated and has project creation permissions. The project creation form is open.
steps: Navigate to the project creation screen,Enter a project name containing special characters and Unicode, e.g., '项目 <Test> & "Alpha" #1 🚀',Fill in all other required fields with valid data,Submit the form
expected_result: The system should either accept the name and correctly store/display it with proper encoding (no data corruption, XSS injection, or display errors), or reject disallowed characters with a clear validation message. The application must not crash or expose a security vulnerability.
rationale: Existing character boundary tests focus on length but not character type. Special characters and Unicode can cause SQL injection, XSS vulnerabilities, encoding errors, or display issues. This edge case validates both security and internationalization robustness.

title: Create project with name at maximum allowed character length
preconditions: Project Manager is authenticated and on the project creation screen. The system has a defined maximum character limit for project name (e.g., 255 characters).
steps: Log in as a Project Manager,Navigate to the new project creation form,Enter a project name exactly at the maximum allowed character limit (e.g., 255 characters),Enter a valid description and start date,Submit the form
expected_result: The system either successfully creates the project with the maximum-length name, or returns a clear validation error if the name exceeds the limit — not a crash, truncation without warning, or ambiguous failure.
rationale: Boundary values on string length are a common source of buffer overflows, silent truncation, or UI/DB mismatches. This is not covered by existing tests which do not address field-length boundaries.

title: Create project with name or description containing only whitespace characters
preconditions: Project Manager is authenticated and on the project creation screen.
steps: Log in as a Project Manager,Navigate to the new project creation form,Enter only whitespace characters (spaces, tabs, newlines) in the project name field,Enter a valid description and start date,Attempt to submit the form,Repeat the test entering whitespace only in the description field while providing a valid name
expected_result: The system treats whitespace-only input as invalid for required fields and displays an appropriate validation error, preventing project creation with a blank effective name or description.
rationale: Whitespace-only input bypasses simple null/empty checks but produces meaningless data. Existing tests cover missing fields but not inputs that appear non-empty yet contain no meaningful content.

title: Attempt to create a project as a non-Project Manager role (authorization boundary)
preconditions: A user with a role other than Project Manager (e.g., Developer, Viewer, or Guest) is authenticated.
steps: Log in as a user without the Project Manager role,Attempt to navigate to or directly call the new project creation endpoint/screen,Fill in all required fields with valid data,Attempt to submit the form or API request
expected_result: The system denies the request with an appropriate authorization error (e.g., HTTP 403 Forbidden or UI access restriction), and no project is created in the system.
rationale: REQ-014 explicitly restricts project creation to Project Managers. No existing test validates that unauthorized roles are actually blocked, leaving a critical authorization gap.

title: Create project with a start date in the past
preconditions: Project Manager is authenticated and on the project creation screen. Current date is known.
steps: Log in as a Project Manager,Navigate to the new project creation form,Enter a valid project name and description,Enter a start date that is significantly in the past (e.g., 1 year ago),Leave the end date empty,Submit the form
expected_result: The system either accepts the past start date (if retrospective project creation is a valid business scenario) or returns a clear validation error indicating the start date cannot be in the past — behavior is consistent and documented, not undefined.
rationale: Existing tests do not address temporal validity of the start date relative to the current date. Allowing or silently rejecting a past start date without clear feedback can lead to data integrity issues or user confusion.

title: Project Manager attempts to add a team member who is already in the project
preconditions: A project exists with at least one team member already assigned. The Project Manager is authenticated and has the appropriate role.
steps: Log in as a Project Manager.,Navigate to the project's team management page.,Attempt to add a user who is already an active member of the project.,Confirm or submit the add action.
expected_result: The system should display a clear error or informational message indicating the user is already a member of the project, and no duplicate entry is created.
rationale: Duplicate membership could cause data integrity issues, permission conflicts, or confusing UI states. This boundary case is not covered by existing tests.

title: Project Manager attempts to add or remove a team member from an archived or inactive project
preconditions: A project exists in an archived or inactive state. The Project Manager is authenticated.
steps: Log in as a Project Manager.,Navigate to a project that has been archived or marked as inactive.,Attempt to add a new team member to the project.,Separately attempt to remove an existing team member from the same project.
expected_result: The system should reject both operations and display an appropriate message indicating that team membership cannot be modified on an archived or inactive project.
rationale: Modifying team membership on closed or archived projects may violate business rules and lead to audit or compliance issues. This state-based edge case is not covered by existing tests.

title: Concurrent requests by two Project Managers to remove the same team member simultaneously
preconditions: A project has exactly two team members. Two Project Managers are authenticated and have access to the same project.
steps: Log in as Project Manager A and Project Manager B in separate sessions.,Both navigate to the team management page of the same project simultaneously.,Both initiate a remove action on the same team member at the same time.,Observe the system's response for both sessions.
expected_result: The system should handle the concurrent requests gracefully — one removal succeeds and the other receives an appropriate error or conflict response. No data corruption or unintended state should occur.
rationale: Race conditions during concurrent modifications can lead to inconsistent data states. This concurrency edge case is not addressed in any existing test.

title: Project Manager attempts to add a team member whose account has been deactivated or suspended
preconditions: A registered user account exists but has been deactivated or suspended by an administrator. The Project Manager is authenticated.
steps: Log in as a Project Manager.,Navigate to the team management page of an active project.,Search for and attempt to add the deactivated or suspended user to the project.,Submit the add action.
expected_result: The system should reject the operation and display a message indicating the user account is inactive or suspended and cannot be added to a project.
rationale: Adding a deactivated user could grant unintended access or create ghost memberships. This differs from the existing 'non-existent user' test case, as the user exists in the system but has a restricted status.

title: Archive a project that is currently being actively edited by another user
preconditions: At least two users exist. User A has permission to archive projects. User B has the project open and is actively editing it (e.g., has unsaved changes or an active edit session).
steps: User B opens a project and begins editing without saving.,Simultaneously, User A navigates to the project list and initiates an archive action on the same project.,User A confirms the archive operation.,Observe the behavior for both User A and User B.
expected_result: The system should either prevent archiving while the project has active sessions and display an appropriate warning, or archive the project and immediately notify User B that the project has been archived, preventing further data loss. Data integrity must be maintained and no silent data corruption should occur.
rationale: Concurrency edge case: archiving while another user has an active edit session is not covered by existing tests and could lead to data inconsistency or silent failures.

title: Verify archived project does not appear in search/filter results under default context
preconditions: At least one project has been successfully archived. The system has a search or filter functionality for projects.
steps: Archive a project with a distinctive name (e.g., 'ArchiveTestProject').,Navigate to the default project list view.,Use the search/filter functionality to search for the archived project by its exact name.,Check if any API endpoints or linked resources (e.g., dashboards, reports) still reference the archived project.
expected_result: The archived project should not appear in default search or filter results. It should only appear if the user explicitly filters for archived projects. No default views, dashboards, or linked resources should surface the archived project without explicit user intent.
rationale: 'Hidden from default project list' may extend to search and filter contexts not explicitly tested. This boundary case ensures the hiding behavior is consistent across all default-context entry points, not just the main list view.

title: Restore an archived project and verify it reappears correctly in the default project list
preconditions: A project has been previously archived. The user has appropriate permissions to restore/unarchive projects.
steps: Navigate to the archived projects view.,Select the archived project and perform an unarchive/restore action.,Navigate to the default project list.,Verify all original project data, metadata, and associations (members, tasks, settings) are intact.
expected_result: The restored project reappears in the default project list with all original data fully preserved and unmodified. No data corruption or loss should occur as a result of the archive-restore cycle.
rationale: The requirement states data must not be deleted on archive. A round-trip test (archive then restore) validates data integrity across the full lifecycle. This is not covered by existing tests which only check preservation during archival, not after restoration.

title: Attempt to archive a project that has dependencies referenced by active projects
preconditions: At least two active projects exist where Project A contains resources, tasks, or data referenced or linked by active Project B.
steps: Confirm Project B has active references or dependencies on Project A (e.g., shared tasks, linked milestones, or shared team members).,Attempt to archive Project A using an account with archive permissions.,Confirm the archive action.,Navigate to Project B and verify its references to Project A.
expected_result: The system should either warn the user that Project A has active dependencies before archiving, or proceed with archiving and gracefully handle dangling references in Project B (e.g., display a clear indicator that the referenced project is archived). No broken links or unhandled null references should appear in active projects.
rationale: Archiving a project with cross-project dependencies is a boundary condition not addressed in existing tests. Hidden data from archived projects could silently break active project workflows if dependency resolution is not handled.

title: Null/Empty End Date Submission
preconditions: User is creating or editing a project with a valid start date already set.
steps: Navigate to the project creation or edit form.,Enter a valid start date (e.g., 2024-06-01).,Leave the end date field empty or null.,Attempt to submit the form.
expected_result: The system should either reject the submission with a clear validation error indicating the end date is required, or handle the null/empty end date gracefully without crashing or silently violating the date constraint.
rationale: Existing tests only cover cases where both dates are provided. A null or empty end date is a common edge case that could bypass date comparison logic and lead to unexpected system behavior or data integrity issues.

title: Invalid Date Format or Non-Date String as End Date
preconditions: User is creating or editing a project with a valid start date set.
steps: Navigate to the project creation or edit form.,Enter a valid start date (e.g., 2024-06-01).,Enter a non-date string or malformed date (e.g., 'abc', '99/99/9999', '2024-13-40') as the end date.,Attempt to submit the form.
expected_result: The system should reject the submission with a clear validation error indicating the end date is invalid, and must not allow the malformed value to be compared against the start date or persisted to the database.
rationale: Type mismatch or malformed date inputs are not covered by existing tests. If the date comparison logic does not validate input types first, it could throw unhandled exceptions, produce incorrect comparisons, or allow invalid data to be stored.

title: Concurrent Updates: Two Users Setting Conflicting Date Ranges Simultaneously
preconditions: Two users (User A and User B) have the same project open for editing simultaneously. The project currently has a valid date range.
steps: User A loads the project edit form and changes the start date to a later date (e.g., 2024-12-01), but does not submit yet.,User B simultaneously loads the same project edit form and changes the end date to an earlier date (e.g., 2024-06-01), submitting successfully.,User A then submits their change.,Check the final persisted start and end dates of the project.
expected_result: The system should detect the conflict through optimistic/pessimistic locking or re-validation at the time of each save, and ensure the final persisted state never results in an end date earlier than the start date. An appropriate conflict or validation error should be surfaced to the user whose change creates the violation.
rationale: Concurrency scenarios are not covered by existing tests. Race conditions between simultaneous edits could result in a persisted project state where the end date is earlier than the start date, even if each individual submission appeared valid at the time of the client-side check.

title: Start Date Changed via API Bypass to Be After Existing End Date
preconditions: A project exists with a valid date range (start: 2024-01-01, end: 2024-06-01). The API endpoint for updating project dates is accessible.
steps: Craft a direct API request (e.g., via curl or Postman) to update only the start date of the project to a date after the existing end date (e.g., 2024-12-01), bypassing the UI form.,Submit the API request with valid authentication credentials.,Observe the API response and check the persisted project dates in the database.
expected_result: The server-side validation should enforce the date constraint at the API layer and reject the request with an appropriate error response (e.g., HTTP 400 with a descriptive message). The project's dates should remain unchanged in the database.
rationale: Existing tests appear to cover UI-level validation only. Server-side enforcement of the date constraint must also be validated to prevent malicious or unintended API calls from creating invalid date ranges by updating only one side of the date pair.

title: Duplicate a project while a concurrent duplication of the same project is in progress
preconditions: A Project Manager is authenticated. An existing project with multiple tasks exists. A second session is simultaneously triggering a duplication of the same project.
steps: Session A: Project Manager initiates duplication of Project X.,Session B: A second Project Manager simultaneously initiates duplication of the same Project X before Session A completes.,Allow both operations to complete.,Inspect the resulting duplicated projects and their task lists for data integrity.
expected_result: Both duplication operations complete independently and successfully, each producing a fully intact duplicate with the complete task list. No data corruption, partial task lists, or deadlocks occur. The original project remains unmodified.
rationale: Concurrent duplication of the same project could cause race conditions, partial writes, or data corruption in the task list if the system does not handle concurrent access properly.

title: Duplicate a project where tasks contain null or empty field values
preconditions: A Project Manager is authenticated. An existing project has tasks where some fields (e.g., description, due date, assignee) are null or empty strings.
steps: Identify a project containing tasks with one or more null/empty fields.,Project Manager initiates the duplication of this project.,Observe the duplication process.,Inspect the task list of the duplicated project for completeness and accuracy.
expected_result: The duplication succeeds without errors. All tasks are copied to the new project, preserving null or empty field values exactly as they are in the original, without substituting defaults or throwing exceptions.
rationale: Null or empty values in task fields are a common boundary condition that can cause unexpected null-pointer exceptions or data transformation errors during the copy process.

title: Duplicate a project immediately before the source project is deleted
preconditions: A Project Manager is authenticated. An existing project with tasks is scheduled for deletion. A duplication request is initiated at nearly the same time as the deletion.
steps: Project Manager initiates duplication of Project X.,Concurrently, an admin or automated process deletes Project X before the duplication transaction completes.,Observe the system response to the duplication request.
expected_result: The system either completes the duplication successfully using a snapshot of the project state at the time duplication was initiated, or returns a clear, user-friendly error message indicating the source project no longer exists. No partial or corrupted duplicate is persisted.
rationale: A race condition between deletion and duplication of the same project could result in a partially duplicated project with an incomplete task list or orphaned records, which is a critical data integrity issue.

title: Duplicate a project when the system has reached the maximum allowed number of projects
preconditions: A Project Manager is authenticated. The system or the user's workspace is at the maximum allowed project capacity limit.
